data.title: Rates Spark: Is Sell America Inc. Now A Thing?
data.description:
By Padhraic Garvey, CFA, and Benjamin Schroeder

US Treasury yields have marched higher again - What's going on?

The impact effect of tariffs was the discount for a recession and more rate cuts, and that combination was deemed a good rationale to buy bonds. It still is, and still could be in months to come. But the more troubling narrative of late is the notion of what we call a "Sell America Inc." risk. It's tough to get a gauge on this, and by the way, it does not have to be selling by foreigners, it can also be from domestics. What we can say with certainty is Treasury prices have been bullied lower in the past few days, and there has been a near-25bp spread underperformance versus German Bunds.

Moreover, the US 10yr swap spread has shot out to 60bp. That's a material cheapening in Treasuries versus the SOFR risk-free rate curve. This is something that we had feared could have happened. That, plus the fiscal deficit plus elevation inflation expectations, was the combination that we initially suggested could drive the 10yr to 5%. We never took that off the table completely, but had reverted to a more bullish tactical call for Treasuries as the recession risk became elevated. See more here.

Clearly, there are potential wild extremes in play here. If we were to slip into recession, there is a path there for yields to revert lower. But the here and now is painting Treasuries as a tainted product, and that's not comfortable territory. And not for the Trump administration that had championed previous falls in Treasury yields as something to cling to as a positive offset from the tariffs narrative that was hurting risk assets. Well, no more, it seems. Treasuries are proving to be a pain trade too.

As a barometer, Tuesday's US 3yr auction tailed badly, by almost 2.5bp. Effectively coming at a concession of that size to the secondary market. The primary dealers also had to take down 20% of the issue, which is high. Treasuries were not trading great into the auction, but the poor outcome gave them another kick. The important thing about the 3yr is it’s a line that global central banks tend to lap up. There was a decent indirect bid into the auction, but not aggressive enough.

And we can't ignore the idle talk of century bond swaps and / or a user fee on Treasuries, which clearly paints a negative underpinning for holders. See more here. As we've noted before, Treasury holders don't want drama. They want simplicity, as a means to seeking haven from risk products when things look bad. The last thing they want is complications. The infamous Mar-a-Largo report is one where such aforementioned complications were heralded as at least up for discussion. Not determinative of the poor 3yr auction, but certainly has been an underlying discussion point that does not help.

There will be nervousness into Wednesday's 10yr auction, especially as this is the benchmark line. The theme here is a troubling one for Treasuries.

As tariffs will start to bite, the focus is on ECB cuts

Broader market tensions did ease somewhat initially, although in the end EUR swap curves still bull-flattened on the day. After all, tariffs will come into force on Wednesday, with no indications of delays or deals to suggest otherwise. It appeared that the market was putting some hope on the growing list of countries wanting to negotiate, which is touted by the Trump administration.

Still, there will be an initial negative impact. The EUR market remains squarely focussed on an April European Central Bank cut. These expectations were confirmed by comments from ECB officials such as Lithuania’s Simkus, though he did push back against the idea of a 50bp cut. Overall, the market is still seeing the ECB cutting to at least 1.75% this year.

In contrast to swaps, the bear-flattening of the Bund curve was a reflection of the swaps spread dynamic reversing - risk sentiment eased from the extremes, and the financial market's plumbing has held up. The 2y Schatz yield rose by 6bp, while the swap rate declined 2bp, 10y Bund yields rose 2bp while swap rates declined by more than 3bp. As a result, the 10y yield sits back at around 5bp above swaps and - if broader markets remain calm - we think it can stabilise in the 5bp to 10bp area near term. We think it is too early for the market to think about the looming supply impact from the German fiscal plans with the tariffs really coming into force now.

Wednesday’s events and market view

There is no data of note scheduled for release in Europe, leaving the focus squarely on the response to the US tariffs, which will come into force on Wednesday. ECB speakers for the day are Knot, Villeroy and Cipollone.

In the US, the main focus will be on the FOMC minutes of the March 19 meeting, a bit outdated but they should confirm the message that the Fed is in no hurry to act for now. The Fed’s Barkin is the only scheduled speaker for the day.

In primary markets, Portugal is slated to reopen 5y and 12y bonds for up to €1.25bn in total. The UK auctions £4.5bn in 5y gilts, while the US Treasury will sell US$39bn in 10y notes.

Content Disclaimer

This publication has been prepared by ING solely for information purposes irrespective of a particular user's means, financial situation or investment objectives. The information does not constitute investment recommendation, and nor is it investment, legal or tax advice or an offer or solicitation to purchase or sell any financial instrument. Read more

Original Post

data.title: Autodesk: Solid Business Trading At The Low End Of Its Trading Band
data.description:
Editor's note: Seeking Alpha is proud to welcome Value Sights as a new contributing analyst. You can become one too! Share your best investment idea by submitting your article for review to our editors. Get published, earn money, and unlock exclusive SA Premium access. Click here to find out more »

Investment summary

My recommendation for Autodesk (NASDAQ:ADSK) is a buy rating. While the macro environment is challenging, I believe ADSK business remains structurally strong. Its products are sticky, and it has a strong network effect that I do not foresee going away anytime soon. The markets that it is exposed to also have strong growth drivers in place. With valuation at the low end of its historical trading band, I think this is a good entry point for investors.

Company description

ADSK offers solutions that help to improve design-centric workflow productivity. Its most famous product is AutoCAD, which is a common platform used by many for 2D/3D modelling purposes. Architecture, Engineering & Construction [AEC] is ADSK’s main vertical, but it has also expanded into verticals like Manufacturing and Media & Entertainment [M&E]. By product family (as of 4Q25, per ADSK filings), AEC is 49% of total revenue, AutoCAD and AutoCAD LT is 25%, manufacturing is 19%, M&E is 5%, and others is 2%. ADSK generates 93% of its revenue from subscriptions.

Industry Landscape

ADSK operates in a very large total addressable market with exposure to multiple long-term secular tailwinds. The two biggest markets for AEC are construction and public infrastructure, with an estimated TAM of ~$45 billion, split between $30 billion for design and $15 billion for build. For reference, ADSK AEC revenue is only ~$2.9 billion in FY25, which implies a market share of <10% (Use ADSK FY24 AEC revenue divided by TAM as shown above).

Although it is virtually impossible to size the TAM, I think management estimates have merit, considering the multiple drivers in place. Firstly, the US is set to undergo a massive reshoring exercise, which has been accelerated by Trump’s tariffs. Secondly, the US housing market is still significantly undersupplied, and this gap has to close eventually. Thirdly, the world is currently in an AI-investment cycle, wherein enterprises and nations are rushing to invest in AI, which means more requirements for building data centers. Lastly, I expect the US public infrastructure spending (the $1.2 trillion Infrastructure Investment and Jobs Act) to be a major growth catalyst over the coming years, especially since these are mega projects that require deep and detailed modeling in the design phase. To achieve all this, more construction is needed, and I expect this to drive up demand for ADSK’s solutions.

Outside of construction, manufacturing is also a big market that shouldn’t be overlooked. The estimated TAM is $42 billion, split between $17 billion in design and $25 billion in Make. A big part of the growth story here, I believe, is the transition to cloud-based solutions and integration of AI.

Regarding the former, there are still many manufacturers still relying on legacy on-premise solutions that work in silos. While such solutions do work, they are not productive relative to cloud-based solutions that are integrated into the organization’s database. For example, a cloud-based solution like ADSK’s Fusion 360 enables the enterprise to facilitate seamless data exchange and real-time collaboration. This is much more productive than on-premise solutions where information is passed through emails and collaborations are done on a lagged basis. Given that in a typical design project there are multiple stakeholders, the overall productivity is simply not great using legacy solutions.

As for AI integration, I expect this to be a very strong growth catalyst that pushes enterprises to adopt solutions like what ADSK offers. To leverage AI capabilities—like using predictive capabilities and automation to facilitate digital twins and simulation—the solutions that enterprises employ must be well integrated across all stakeholders to ensure data fidelity. And this is something that on-premise legacy solutions cannot offer.

Competitive Advantages

ADSK’s biggest competitive advantage is the inherent switching cost of its products and solutions. First of all, ADSK serves a mission-critical function in the entire planning and design workflow (the team literally needs ADSK to plan and design everything from the ground up). Because this workflow process also involves external stakeholders (building contractors, etc.), it means that ADSK is very well embedded into the entire workflow process, making it incredibly hard to just rip and replace. Supposed one were to do so, it would need to replace all the design file formats, ensure nothing is missing, make sure all stakeholders know how to collaborate using the new solution, etc., and these significantly increase execution risk.

Additionally, it takes a lot of effort, time, and cost to fully train an entire department/organization to be well versed with ADSK’s solution. Because of that, in many cases, the cost of retraining teams, onboarding to new systems, and reworking file standards far outweighs any software cost savings (suppose there was an intention to switch out of ADSK). If the entire team only consists of very few members and there isn’t much external collaboration, it may be possible to switch. But in a large enterprise setting where design teams often consist of more than a few employees, it is a lot more complex to switch.

Another big advantage is its network effect. Take AutoCAD in the AEC market, for instance. As I noted above, design and construction projects typically got a whole mix of stakeholders involved: architects, civil engineers, contractors, and regulators. And there is a critical need to exchange information across a shared workflow. If everyone’s using AutoCAD, the entire information exchange is frictionless (no time lost to file conversion, no retraining across tools, and less human error). The idea is that the more people widely adopt AutoCAD, the more attractive it is for new users, ass new learners or new entrants into the industry will look to adopt AutoCAD (since everyone else is using it, it will be easier to collaborate). Per 6sense, AutoCAD has the highest market share at ~39%, which is ~3x larger than its closest peer.

There is also another reason why ADSK network effects should continue to get better—its strategic focus on providing free education. While some may see this as a cost to the company, I see this as a strong funnel. When students graduate, the only design software they’re fluent in is likely going to be ADSK’s. And when they join firms, they bring that knowledge with them, which further enhances ADSK's network effect. So the cycle repeats: more users make collaboration easier, which draws in even more users. This scale compounds over time, and I believe it’s one of the biggest reasons why ADSK has remained as the market leader.

4Q25 financials

ADSK financials also highlight the strength of the business. Looking at the recent 4Q25 quarter, total revenue came in at $1.63 billion, up 12% y/y in constant currency [CC], wherein subscription revenue grew 13% y/y to $1.53 billion, and billings grew 31% y/y to $1.45 billion. This marked the 8th consecutive quarter of low-teens constant currency growth, which I think speaks well of the underlying demand. ADSK was also able to expand margins roughly 160 bps y/y to ~37%, helping it to deliver 4Q25 net income of $498 million, bringing the FY25 net income to ~$1.84 billion. While management guided FY26 revenue growth of 8–9% y/y growth, which is lower than the low-teens growth in recent quarters, I think this is a cautious guide given the uncertain macro outlook for this year—especially with the tariffs being implemented. I am pleased that management is not guiding aggressively for growth, as it gives it room to beat its own guidance. For reference, ADSK has historically beaten its own guidance—almost every quarter for the past 5 years.

Valuation

In my valuation model, I used management’s FY26 guidance to project FY26 revenue, but I modeled growth to accelerate back to low teens in FY27 and FY28. My view is that the long-term growth drivers are still very much intact. As the macro environment recovers, we should see demand recover accordingly as businesses become more confident. Along with my assumption that ADSK can sustain its net margin at ~31% (same as FY26 guidance), I forecast ADSK to generate ~$2.6 billion in net income. I used a 25x NTM P/E to value ADSK, which I think is conservative given that the historical trading band of ADSK is between 25x and 30x. Inputting these assumptions, I see a share price upside of ~23%.

Thoughts on Starboard activist

ADSK is currently facing pressure from activist investor Starboard Value, which recently nominated directors ahead of the June AGM. Per their letter, they believe ADSK can achieve 45% EBIT margins by FY28, which is an ~800bps uplift, through cost efficiencies and better execution. While I think their target may be aggressive, I think this is positive for shareholders. If anything, it forces management to be on the ball to expand margin. And this represents upside potential in my model, as I did not model in margin expansion in my forecasted periods. I think ADSK management is doing what they can do to deliver expectations – they have a new CFO in place. Hence, on an overall basis, I see this as a rather healthy “forcing function” that could help ADSK realize higher margins.

Risk

ADSK is undergoing a shift to more automated, self-service go-to-market sales motions. While this has its upside, it could lead to slower demand if not implemented properly (some users may want more customized solutions that the self-service model cannot address, thus leading to such users looking for alternative solutions). As much as there are strong positive growth drivers in place, ultimately, the macroeconomy has to move in the right direction for demand to come through. If Trump’s tariffs cause a recession, ADSK will be heavily impacted regardless.

Final thoughts

I give a buy rating. While near-term growth may moderate slightly, I think the long-term setup looks solid. I believe ADSK still has plenty of room to grow its share in both AEC and manufacturing, and it is very positive that these markets are also growing, driven by secular tailwinds such as reshoring, housing undersupply, investments in AI infrastructure, and public infrastructure investments. At current levels, I think the upside is compelling.

data.title: Bank M&A Activity Rebounds In March, Pushing Q1 2025 Total Deal Value To $1.61B
data.description:
Thirty-four US bank deals worth a combined $1.61 billion were announced in the first quarter this year, marking the highest first quarter total by aggregate deal value since 2021, according to S&P Global Market Intelligence data.

By comparison, first quarter announced deals totaled $1.53 billion in 2022, $433.8 million in 2023 and $796.2 million in 2024.

Fifteen of the quarter's 34 deals were announced in March, with many deal announcements delayed for buyers and sellers waiting for transaction conditions to improve.

Nashville, Tennessee-based FB Financial Corp. (FBK) announced its acquisition of Anniston, Alabama-based Southern States Bancshares Inc. (SSBK) for $380.7 million on the last day of the quarter. The deal value-to-tangible common equity ratio was 156.6%. The transaction was the largest and fourth-most expensive US bank M&A deal announced so far in 2025. It marked FB Financial's return to dealmaking, more than five years after the bank announced its $623.1 million purchase of Franklin Financial Network Inc. in January 2020.

The reverse merger transaction of Seattle-based HomeStreet Inc. (HMST) and Walnut Creek, California-based Mechanics Bank (OTC:MCHB) was also announced March 31. With $8.12 billion in total assets as of the deal announcement, Homestreet is the largest bank to be targeted so far in 2025. The transaction will expand Mechanics Bank's West Coast footprint and create a combined company with $23 billion in assets.

Previously, Denver-based FirstSun Capital Bancorp (FSUN) announced its purchase of HomeStreet in January 2024, but the banks terminated their merger agreement after regulators blocked the deal.

CU-bank deals announced in March

On March 12, Joliet, Illinois-based NuMark CU said it will acquire substantially all assets and liabilities of Lemont, Illinois-based Lemont National Bank. The transaction marks the fifth bank target headquartered in Illinois this year, making it the most targeted state.

Birmingham, Alabama-based Legacy Community FCU announced its purchase of Cullman, Alabama-based First Community Bank of Cullman on March 18.

The two deals are expected to close in 2025, subject to regulatory approvals.

Bank deals by state, region

On March 11, Bar Harbor, Maine-based Bar Harbor Bankshares (BHB) announced its purchase of Woodsville, New Hampshire-based Guaranty Bancorp Inc. (OTC:GUAA) for $39.9 million, marking the first New Hampshire bank targeted this year.

On March 17, Doraville, Georgia-based MetroCity Bankshares Inc. (MCBS) announced the acquisition of in-state peer First IC Corp. (OTC:FIEB) for $206.0 million, in the fourth-largest US bank M&A deal announced so far in 2025 and the largest deal involving a minority depository institution as a buyer since 2019.

The Midwest has been the year's most targeted region, with 13 target banks accounting for almost 40% of all M&A transactions announced. The Southeast had six target banks, making it the second-most targeted region.

M&A outlook

US bank M&A activity in the first quarter was 'far from the wave' that many expected, but deals are closing faster, according to Hovde Group analyst Brendan Nosal.

First quarter stock market volatility paused several bank deals, but investment bankers counseled patience, saying bank deals will likely be announced later in the year. However, advisers cautioned that US bank M&A activity may come to a halt if tariff-induced stock plunges continue.

On April 3, Portsmouth, Virginia-based TowneBank (TOWN) announced its $202.1 million purchase of Hampton, Virginia-based Old Point Financial Corp. (OPOF), extending the surge of $100 million-plus deal announcements in early spring.

Original Post

Editor's Note: The summary bullets for this article were chosen by Seeking Alpha editors.

Editor's Note: This article discusses one or more securities that do not trade on a major U.S. exchange. Please be aware of the risks associated with these stocks.

data.title: Global Ship Lease: Deeply Undervalued
data.description:
Global Ship Lease (NYSE:GSL) owns one of the world's largest container ship parks. The main source of income is to rent ships for cargo lines under a long-term fixed rate agreement.

Although GSL has been generating hundreds of millions for the third year in a row, during this time, it aggressively reduced debts, bought its shares, and increased dividends. Nevertheless, the market is still looking at it as a cyclical player approaching the peak of profitability. GSL is currently selling with a P/E (TTM) ratio below 2.5, and the dividend is more than 10%.

Overview

The GSL fleet consists of 71 vessels with a total capacity of over 400,000 DWT. The fleet is distinguished by the fact that most of its ships are medium-sized, perfect for regional routes where mega ships cannot moor. The average age of ships is around 17.5 years, but the 9,000 DWT new ECO-class ships have helped improve the efficiency of the fleet. It is important to note that the company has ~89% of 2025 and ~ 66% of 2026 vessel operating days under contract, which ensures a stable income flow.

Sector and Macro Trends

Global container shipping has recently encountered growth issues. In 2024, cargo volumes grew by more than 7%, which was a strong signal that global trade had recovered from the pandemic. On the other hand, geopolitical conflicts, especially in the Red Sea, forced ships to choose longer routes, bypassing Suez.

Meanwhile, the GSL segment, which focuses on medium-sized vessels, has a structural advantage. These types of ships are the most important in regional routes, among ports that cannot accept large ships. In addition, the construction of container ships in such a segment is limited, and many older ships are approaching the age limit (20-25 years), so it is expected that the supply in this segment may even shrink in the next few years.

From 2023, the new classification of EEXI and CII emissions, which requires slower sailing (slow steaming), increases the need for additional fleets. GSL, with more effective ships, can use this regulatory pressure as a competitive advantage.

Financials

Financially, GSL is perfectly prepared. For example, in 2024, revenue was $711 million, EBITDA exceeded $495 million, and net profit reached $344 million, nearly $10 per share. Such numbers are particularly impressive in terms of the fact that GSL has one of the most stable portfolios in the whole sector. It is even more surprising that the company not only generates such profits but also aggressively reduces debt from $823 million in 2023 to $691 million in 2024. Even the Debt/EBITDA indicator fell to just 1.1x, while the rating agencies Moody's and S&P have already increased the GSL credit ratings to BB+ / Ba2.

The dividends currently reach $1.80 per year, and the dividend yield is about 9.36%. Even at this level, dividends are more than attractive, especially when the payout ratio is only 17.27%. This means that the company pays only part of its profit by leaving space for both dividends and reinvestment or debt reduction.

Valuation

Valuations show a great distrust of the market that is not based on fundamental indicators:

  • P/E GAAP (TTM): 1.99
  • EV/EBITDA: 2.65
  • P/b: 0.47
  • EV/Sales: 1.69

This leads to the conclusion that the market has already priced in the worst scenario. Even if the profits were reduced by 50%, GSL would remain one of the most profitable companies in the entire shipping sector. So GSL potentially has 100+ % growth upside.

Possible Catalysts

Selling older vessels. Like they did in 2024, they sold three old ships for a price twice their balance value. This proves the point that the assets in the balance sheet are not evaluated properly by investors. Further similar sales would increase cash and may initiate additional dividends or stock repurchases.

If the company maintains a debt level <1.0x EBITDA and will continue paying dividends, it is likely that in 2025, the ratings will be increased to BBB-. This would make it cheaper to borrow and may increase the interest of institutional investors.

Risks

Although approximately 89% of vessel operating days in 2025 and 66% in 2026 are already secured through long-term contracts, these agreements will eventually expire. If by 2026, the market gets severely weakened, GSL may need to lease the ships at lower rates. However, the construction level of new medium-sized container ships is historically low, so even if the economy gets weakened due to demand, the rates can remain stable.

Global trade continues to face instability due to geopolitical conflicts - especially in the Red Sea region, and let's call it increasing protectionism. Higher rates or regional conflicts can affect container flows. However, the GSL business model avoids short-term risks, the company receives revenue for ship rentals rather than for the cargo transported, and the main customers are large linear shipping companies. Moreover, the disruption in the Red Sea has increased ton-mile demand, which could support average charter rates over the medium term.

Although GSL controls quite an efficient and on-demand fleet, its average age of ships is around 17.5 years. If the requirements of the IMO (International Maritime Organization) for carbon emissions are quickly tightened, older ships may be forcibly retired from the premature life. However, in 2024, sales of ships suggest that GSL’s assets are undervalued on the balance sheet. The company sold three older vessels for $54.5 million, despite a combined book value of just $24.9 million.

Summary

Global Ship Lease is a good company with fixed profits, high efficiency, and clear risk control. Due to historically low valuations and structural demand in its segments, the company has all the conditions for a significant increase in share price in the coming years.

Even if the sector was in trouble, GSL's strong balance and long contracts allow investors to wait out cyclical fluctuations.

data.title: AT&T: A Safe Harbour
data.description:
In times of stormy seas, captains seek safe harbours. The same applies to stocks and investors. When the markets are turbulent and prices are falling, defensive stocks outperform. AT&T (NYSE:T) meets several criteria that currently make the stock a safe haven for investors.

Investment thesis

Good old T has put its past experiments and forays into the streaming and advertising business behind it.

Since then, it has delivered a convincing performance with stable business development. The strong cash flow supports the operating business, pays the dividend (even if the dividend cut still hurts) and allows a reduction of the balance sheets that will give the company more financial flexibility in the future, which the company can use for share buybacks, for example.

In addition, the company is a pure domestic play that generates the majority of its sales in its home market of the US. This should protect the business from at least the immediate effects of the tariff dispute, although indirect effects due to a recession and the resulting low consumer spending cannot be ruled out.

Overall, I consider the stock to be one of the few ‘safe’ havens for cash-flow-oriented investors in these turbulent times. Even if further price losses in line with the market are possible, the overall performance should be better. During this time, investors benefit from a secure dividend yield of over 4%.

AT&T is a defensive play

Since the AT&T stock bottomed out in the summer of 2023, AT&T has significantly outperformed the S&P in the last few quarters.

So the stock has momentum that continued into the first quarter of 2025 when the S&P 500 was already faltering somewhat.

This defensive character has continued with the onset of the stock market crash. So far, AT&T has been far more defensive than the broader market.

Outperformance is based on several factors

The outperformance is based on several factors.

On the one hand, AT&T has returned to its roots with its lean businesses in mobility and broadband services and has been posting good results for many quarters.

In Q4, phone subscribers grew from 71.3 million to 72.7 million (including 1.7 million postpaid phone net additions), while ARPU rose from $56.23 to $56.72. Fiber ARPU also rose from $68.5 to $71.71 (4.7% YoY), while Fiber subscribers rose by 1 million to 9.3 million. As a result, Fiber revenue grew by 17.8%, contributing significantly to the 7.8% growth in broadband revenue. Overall, AT&T's Q4 performance marked six consecutive quarters of positive broadband net adds.

As a result, AT&T ended FY 2024 either in line with or ahead of its own guidance on key metrics, such as adj. EPS and revenue growth.

What I see as a shareholder and investor in AT&T as very positive is the low churn rate and the reliability of the services. Postpaid phone churn was 0.85%, which the company says is a best-in-class figure.

We also expect to lead the industry in postpaid phone churn for the 14th time in the last 16 quarters and to lead the industry on an annual basis for the fourth straight year. This is an impressive winning run in wireless. (Source: 4Q earnings call)

It's clear that fiber is the best broadband alternative technology available and this was validated by Ookla just last week, when they named AT&T Fiber America's fastest Internet with the most reliable speeds. (Source: 4Q earnings call)

In addition, investors are finally seeing light at the end of the balance sheet tunnel at AT&T.

For example, AT&T was able to charge Warner Bros. Discovery with $43 billion of debt from AT&T, marking the beginning of a period of significant debt reduction. In the past quarters, AT&T has continued to reduce debt to the delight of investors and thanks to a strong free cash flow of $2.57 per share, reducing net debt by $8.8 billion in FY 2024. As a result, the ratio of net debt to adjusted EBITDA was 2.7 at the end of FY 2024.

AT&T is therefore very close to the target of 2.5x, which the company should achieve in 2025. This is already having a positive impact on cash flow generation when it comes to interest expenses. Cash interest paid in 2024 was $7.13bn, the lowest since 2017.

Modest growth ahead

If you are looking for a fly in the ointment, then it is probably the rather weak guidance for FY 2025 in terms of profit. Due to higher depreciation and amortization expenses in 2025, management expects an adjusted EPS guidance of $1.97 to $2.07 for 2025 (2024: $2.54). Analysts also expect an average EPS decline of 7.5%.

However, investors should note that AT&T still has a high CAPEX that will only materialise over the next five to ten years. Thus, in 2025, AT&T will still have CAPEX in the $22 billion range, with a focus on modernizing its wireless network and fiber services.

Our investment in 5G and fiber is fueling this sustained growth. This is why we're so focused on building a durable franchise for the long-term. Our deliberate and balanced approach to capital allocation is putting us on a strong foundation to deliver attractive returns for years to come. With about $22 billion in capital investment last year, we again invested at the top of the industry as we continue to focus on building the largest, highest capacity, lowest marginal cost, converged broadband network in the country. (Source: 4Q earnings call)

In my view, investments in the fiber segment, in particular, make sense. For one thing, AT&T has an edge here, with 4 out of 10 fiber customers using AT&T. In addition, the expansion of the fiber offering allows customers to be brought onto the fiber services. AT&T reports here that these converged customers are ‘highly valuable and durable with longer customer lives’ (Source: 4Q earnings call). Therefore, I can live with the CAPEX, especially since AT&T is simultaneously reducing its debt.

Plus, the medium-term outlook is more decisive. Analysts and management expect a double-digit CAGR from 2027. The share buyback program announced for the second half of 2025 (which I will discuss in more detail below) will also have a positive impact on EPS growth.

What investors get for their money

With AT&T, investors are getting a company that is pursuing a clear operational strategy with a core business and is increasingly getting rid of the shadows of the past (DIRECTV/TPG transaction), including debt.

In particular, achieving a net debt / adjusted EBITDA target ratio of 2.5 will free up cash flow that the company can use in a shareholder-friendly manner. Over the next three years, AT&T will return $40bn to shareholders, including approximately $10 billion during the second half of 2025 with an already approved share buyback. That's 50% of the overall capacity for about $20 billion in share repurchases.

Despite these expenditures, AT&T is still expected to have an additional $10 billion in incremental financial flexibility, which it says it will use opportunistically. Management explicitly mentions debt reduction or ‘further capital returns to shareholders,’ which I believe could be an indication of rising dividends.

Speaking of dividends.

Yes, the dividend with a yield of 4.2% is rather at the lower end of the historical yield. On the other hand, the distribution of $1.11 per share in FY2024 was easily covered by the free cash flow of $2.54 per share. Therefore, I currently see no reason to believe that the dividend is at risk. Rather, I see the possibility of slight increases, at the latest in 2026.

Even though the current adj. P/E ratio of 11.8 is above the average of recent years, the multiple expansion is justified in my view, given that AT&T's operating performance is now better than it has been in the last ten years. The anticipated EPS improvements in 2027 with an EPS CAGR of 10%+ and forward, as well as the share buyback programs, should give the share price stability and also allow for higher multiples.

In addition, AT&T generates the majority of its revenues in the US. Revenues in the US of $116bn are only offset by revenues from Mexico worth $4.28bn. The exposure of AT&T Services to tariff disputes is, therefore, very low. AT&T is thus a relatively safe haven in these times of turmoil.

Of course, there are risks in the event of a recession caused by the tariff dispute, which could lead to a decline in consumer spending. Above all, this could make it more difficult for customers to convert to fiber services. CAPEX could also increase because even though AT&T is a domestic player, it still relies on imported technologies or components. Key technologies for 5G infrastructure or components for fibre-optic expansion are globalised and cannot be substituted at short notice.

While these risks cannot be dismissed, they affect virtually all major companies in a globalised world. And, of course, an investment in AT&T does not protect investors from the fact that the share price may fall in line with the market. However, this risk is currently faced by all equities. I expect AT&T to outperform the market in the coming months and quarters, precisely because many shareholders may increasingly seek refuge in domestic players like AT&T with a solid balance sheet (I'll say that about AT&T), a secure dividend and a reasonable valuation.

AT&T is, therefore, a buy for me with a short- and mid-term horizon.

data.title: Nvidia Hasn't Bottomed, But It's A Buy Nonetheless (Technical Analysis)(Rating Upgrade)
data.description:
Intro

Since my hold rating back in January, Nvidia Corporation (NASDAQ:NVDA) has declined over 35% as the stock has plunged along with the markets. In the below analysis, I determine that the technicals are heavily bearish, and so further declines in the stock are likely from here. However, that doesn't stop me from upgrading Nvidia to a buy rating, as the stock is now too cheap to ignore. In the fundamental analysis, I determine the business remains resilient despite slowing growth. Furthermore, the P/S ratio has contracted significantly to now far below average levels, while revenue growth is expected to remain at healthy levels. Therefore, I believe investors should take advantage of the technical weakness and undervaluation to buy a fundamentally strong company, even though there may be continued near-term weakness due to fear in global markets.

Technical Analysis

The Basics

Trends, Support & Resistance

Nvidia stock was in a downward channel from the start of January to the start of this month. On the most recent trading day, the stock broke beneath the lower channel line as a signal that the downtrend may be accelerating. In addition to the two channel lines' resistance, there are three more levels worth mentioning here. The nearest one is at around 102, where the first of two consecutive gap downs occurred. This level along with the 107 level could be significant resistance moving forward. Do note that the bearish volume so far this month has not been above to eclipse the volume peak in January. Lastly, there is also resistance at around 120. This level was support back in late January and was resistance for a large part of March. In this timeframe, there is no support for the stock, and so further losses are possible from here.

Moving Averages & Indicators

The 50-day SMA has remained above the 200-day SMA for the past six months, but the gap between them has been shrinking. This signals long term bullish momentum is on the decline. In addition, a death cross may occur in the near future. For the EMAs, there was a bearish crossover back in late February after the 9 day EMA only spent a brief period above the 21 day EMA. This was a signal that the near term had turned bearish again. Currently, the EMAs' deviation continues to build as the stock plunges.

The stock most recently experienced resistance at the Bollinger Bands midline in late March, confirming the downtrend for Nvidia. On the two previous trading days, the stock broke beneath the lower band, and so it may be oversold. For the MACD, it recently pulled away from its signal line, reflecting the increase in near term bearish sentiment. It has also hit a six-month low to confirm the sharp declines in the stock. The RSI just dipped below the 30 level, showing that the bears are in strong control. Like, the MACD, it has also hit six-month lows, demonstrating extraordinary weakness. Lastly, the stochastics had a bearish crossover back in late March and since then, both lines broke beneath the 50 level. The %K has, in fact, entered the 20 zone as another indication of heavy bearish control.

Takeaway

From this technical analysis, I believe it is clear that things are quite ugly for Nvidia as it plunges along with the market. The chart shows an accelerating downtrend and heavy resistance above. The moving averages are overall bearish, with the SMAs showing receding long term bullish sentiment and the EMAs reflecting the major near term weakness in the stock. There are also not many good signs in the indicators, as bearish signals are also abundant there.

Fibonacci

Time Zones

The Fibonacci Time Zone has been quite accurate for predicting the reversals in the stock recently. After the 13-day mark, the stock reversed to the upside, while it reversed to the downside after the 21-day mark. Most recently, Nvidia staged a rally right on the 34th day, and so clearly the stock is responding to this technical tool. The next significant day is the 55th, which will occur in the near future. Therefore, I believe investors can expect major price movement and volatility at around the middle of April.

Projection

Using the early January peak, the March low, and the retracement to around 120, the Fibonacci Projection has provided key support zones if the stock continues to decline. On the previous trading day, Nvidia seems to have found support at the 61.8% level, and so this area in the low 90s could be support again. The next support level indicated here is at the 100% level, which would be in the low 70s. Therefore, in my view, if the stock breaches 90 it may be a while before it finds support again from the Fibonacci Projection.

Ichimoku Cloud (Advanced)

Firstly, the stock is now far beneath the cloud as it has plunged recently, showing that there is strong overall bearishness in the stock currently. Leading Span A broke beneath Leading Span B back in mid-March to show bearish sentiment, and it seems that the stock most recently experienced resistance at the cloud in February. The Conversion line crossed below the Base line at the end of January, showing that near term momentum had shifted bearish for Nvidia. In addition, the Lagging Span's gap with the stock price in mid-February is substantial, confirming the strength in the downtrend. Moving forward, both Leading Span A and B are continuing to fall as the cloud continues to move downward. Therefore, the stock may experience obstacles if it were to attempt a rebound from here. Bearish pressure is likely to continue.

Fundamental Analysis

Earnings

Quarterly Results

Nvidia reported their FY2025 Q4 earnings back in late February and showed respectable results overall. Revenue was reported to be $39.3 billion, up 78% YoY while adjusted EPS was $0.89, up 71% YoY. Both figures beat expectations with revenue beating by $1.19 billion and EPS beating by $0.04, showing strong performance relative to consensus estimates. However, as shown in the chart above, revenue growth has continued to slow from peak levels seen in early calendar 2024. Nonetheless, it remains at around average levels compared to past years. For cash from operations, growth has continued to slowdown, but its $16.6 billion reported for the figure is still a healthy increase over the prior year period's $11.5 billion. Lastly, there has been quite some discussion over Nvidia's margins, but overall adjusted gross margin continues to be at impressive levels as it is still at 73.5% after a YoY decline of 3.2%. Overall, while there is a slowdown in growth, I would say the fundamentals of the business remain strong.

Guidance

The company provided guidance for their FY2026 Q1. They are expecting revenues to be $43.0 billion at the midpoint. With FY2025 Q1 revenues being $26.0 billion, this guidance implies YoY growth of 65.4%, a moderate slowdown from Q4's 78% growth rate. This is a continuation of the downtrend in growth but overall, an over 65% revenue growth rate is still no slouch as the business continues to be robust. However, they project that adjusted gross margin for the quarter will be at 71% at the midpoint, a decrease from the prior year period's 78.9%. This shows that the pricing power of the business may be decreasing by a moderate amount. As a whole, guidance was a bit weaker than desired, but in my view, both revenue and gross margins are still expected to be at healthy levels. As the company grows and its growth base increases, it is no surprise that there are moderations in growth and so overall, the fundamentals remain solid for Nvidia.

Valuation

Price To Sales Ratio [P/S]

The P/S ratio has shown major contraction from its peak in mid-calendar 2023 and even from mid-2024 levels. It currently has a reading of 17.93, representing far below average levels relative to the past three years of data. I believe this multiple shows undervaluation in Nvidia stock. As discussed above, revenue growth has slowed for Q4, but it remains at around average levels From their Q1 guidance, growth is expected to moderate further to the mid-60s percentage range. However, that would still only be a slightly below average level of growth while the P/S ratio is currently at very low levels in relation to Nvidia's trading history. This leads me to believe that the stock is undervalued amid the overwhelming fear in the markets currently. As of now, the Information Technology sector has a median P/S of only 2.43, but I believe that this comparison is not very meaningful for a couple of reasons. Firstly, Nvidia's margins continue to be impressive, and so a higher multiple is no surprise. In addition, the company continues to hold a commanding position in AI infrastructure, and so the premium to "peers" is likely justified at this point.

Risks

Clearly, there are risks to Nvidia's business amid an uncertain environment. The elephant in the room is, of course, President Trump's tariffs. While it is said that the U.S.'s tariffs on Taiwan excludes semiconductors, the resulting poor growth in the global economy has the potential of slowing the demand of GPUs for the buildout of AI infrastructure. In my view, hyper-scalers like Google and Meta are in a strong position to weather economic headwinds and so AI CAPEX may not decrease by a significant amount as the companies continue to fight for dominance in a highly competitive AI market. There are reports that Microsoft may be changing its data center strategy, but overall, there is nothing to suggest that the AI boom is at risk of ending at this point. Another risk would be further export restrictions on high performance GPUs to China. The Trump Administration is widely known to be weighing even more restrictive regulations that may impact Nvidia's China business, but I expect that the company will be able to quickly build complaint chips to preserve the growth in that market. Overall, there are certainly risks to Nvidia's business at this point, but overall, as discussed above, the fundamentals remain very strong and are in a good position to withstand the challenging macro-environment.

Conclusion

In the above analysis, it was determined that the technicals are severely bearish currently. Both the basic technicals and the Ichimoku Cloud indicate that further weakness is likely. However, I believe now is not a bad time to buy into the stock. For the fundamentals, most recent earnings and guidance show that the business remains healthy despite some moderation in growth. The P/S ratio's major contraction suggests that the company is undervalued as revenue growth remains near average levels. Therefore, even though there may be further near term weakness in the stock, I believe investors won't regret picking up Nvidia at current levels as the risk-reward setup is attractive at this point.

data.title: Apple: Beware The Lollapalooza Effect
data.description:
Really big effects, lollapalooza effects, will often come only from large combinations of factors. - Charlie Munger

It is difficult to think of anyone who has ever had a better understanding about how society and the economy work than Charlie Munger. He took the time to understand the financials, the incentives, and the psychology behind how society operates. One particularly interesting mental model that he used was the "lollapalooza effect", which he used to describe situations where a confluence of factors came together to deliver an incredibly powerful impact. This impact could be positive, or negative, and the magnitude often takes most observers by surprise. It could be said that Berkshire Hathaway's impressive returns are the result of the "lollapalooza effect" of the combined skills and wisdom of Warren Buffett, Charlie Munger, the strong U.S. economic expansion during the time they were investing, and the added leverage from their insurance operations, among other factors.

It appears that Apple (NASDAQ:AAPL) (TSX:AAPL:CA) might experience a "lollapalooza effect", but of the negative kind, as a result of a number of negative factors that are likely to impact the company in the coming years. First, most of the products that the company sells are discretionary, with consumers having the option to postpone purchases if their economic situation deteriorates. They are also extremely reliant on overseas manufacturing, with parts and assembly mostly done in Asian countries. If tariffs remain in place, Apple is likely to experience higher production costs that the company will either have to absorb and see profit margins deteriorate, or pass the price increase to customers, which would likely reduce sales considerably. In foreign markets, Apple could also be impacted by retaliatory tariffs, for example Germany and France are considering targeting digital services, or even consumer backlash could lead customers to buy other brands as is happening in China.

All of these is happening at a time when Apple was already struggling to deliver meaningful revenue growth, and where consumer weakness was becoming evident. The added stress from tariffs is likely to send the U.S. economy into recession, as we recently discussed in another article, and this time it will be extremely difficult for the Fed to come to the rescue, as both of its mandates will be under pressure. With the ballooning government deficit and the inflationary impact from tariffs putting pressure on the Fed to increase rates, while higher unemployment and declining economic growth needing the help of lower interest rates. Recession risk has significantly increased, with the likes of Goldman Sachs' (GS) and JPMorgan's (JPM) racing to increase the probabilities of a recession in the next twelve months. Selling more iPhones is going to get tricky at a time when some consumers are even resorting to buy now, pay later financing for their DoorDash (DASH) orders.

Financials

For almost three years now, Apple's revenue and earnings have basically stalled. In fact, revenue growth has barely been above the inflation rate in recent quarters. The one bright spot has been the company's operating margin, which has seen a meaningful increase, but this has failed to translate to large earnings per share (EPS) increases, partly as a result of increased stock-based compensation.

Innovation Decline

Even more concerning, it appears Apple is no longer the innovation powerhouse it used to be under Steve Jobs. The company has failed to deliver on some of its most promising efforts, including the "Project Titan" Apple car. The company spent over a decade and billions of dollars, with little to show for it in the end. At the same time, companies with significantly less resources like Xiaomi (OTCPK:XIACY) basically accomplished Apple's vision with a fraction of the resources and in record time. Xiaomi is expected to sell over 350,000 of its Smart EVs in 2025, and that could have been Apple.

These cars are not only electric and connected to the Xiaomi ecosystem, but are rapidly having autonomous driving features added. These include automated parking capabilities and traffic light real-time alerts. Meanwhile, Apple is failing to even get AI to interpret news and messages correctly. A good example is one case where a user reported that a text from his mother reading “That hike almost killed me!” had been turned into “Attempted suicide, but recovered.” To be fair, trying to summarize group chats is not an easy task, which might justify the summary "Rude comments exchanged". This has prompted articles saying that Apple's AI is anything but intelligent, and the company admitting errors and shaking up its AI executive ranks.

Stock-based Compensation

Despite spending ever-increasing amounts on stock-based compensation, Apple has failed to retain some key talent. This includes the departure of legendary Apple designer Jony Ive in 2019, and more generally, the company sliding in the "Best Place to Work" rankings. Apparently, part of the reason is related to the return-to-work policy, but it probably does not help when the most ambitious engineering projects like the electric car project see multiple leadership changes and end up canceled.

One thing to notice about Apple's stock-based compensation is that it has not only increased in absolute terms, which could be justified by its increased market capitalization, but also as a percentage of its quarterly revenue.

Future Outlook

In the near term, Apple might see a boost to sales, with customers panic buying iPhones and other Apple products before prices increase as a result of tariffs. Some analysts are even speculating we could see $2,300 iPhones in a worst-case scenario. This would no doubt reduce demand and have a significant negative impact on Apple's revenue and profitability. The company also has a lot of work to do to get its VR and AI strategy right.

These developments are starting to be reflected in analyst earnings revisions, with 26 downward revisions in the last 3 months, compared to only 10 upward revisions according to tracking by Seeking Alpha. If current tariffs remain in place, we believe earnings estimates will have to be revised down more meaningfully, especially should other countries retaliate with their own tariffs on U.S. products and services.

Valuation

Despite the considerable revenue and profit growth deceleration, Apple's valuation is meaningfully above its ten-year average multiples for Price/Sales and Price/Earnings. Adding the increased recession and tariff risks, we find it extremely difficult to justify the current valuation, which is meaningfully above the company's historical average. This can also be seen in the extremely low current dividend yield, roughly half the 10-year average.

Risks

While we believe Apple is likely to remain profitable even during the current environment, and we do not think it is a good short idea, we do believe the high valuation and stalling growth means a high likelihood that the company will underperform the market going forward. The biggest risk we see for Apple is the lollapalooza effect of a number of negative factors. These include stalling revenue and profit growth, impacts from higher costs from U.S. tariffs and potential retaliation from other countries, signs that innovation from the company is declining, and rapidly increasing risks of a U.S. recession.

Conclusion

It is clear that multiple headwinds are adding up for Apple, and this is being reflected in declining earnings estimates. While it is difficult to precisely forecast the impact that tariffs, the potential retaliation, and a U.S. recession would have on the company, it is important to remember that most of its products remain discretionary purchases. Even before the current developments, Apple was already delivering disappointing revenue and profit growth, very poor results from its innovation initiatives, and an elevated valuation.

Editor's Note: This article discusses one or more securities that do not trade on a major U.S. exchange. Please be aware of the risks associated with these stocks.

data.title: TLT: An Unique Asymmetrical Opportunity
data.description:
The stock market is fragile, there's no doubt about that. However, few market commentators have highlighted the contemporaneous support for the bond market. While the S&P 500 slid by over 10% in the two trading sessions following President Trump's reciprocal tariff announcement, treasury bonds gained traction, suggesting the U.S. isn't totally uninvestable. Instead, asset class rotation seems like a viable solution.

In our view, long-term bonds are a steady option, which is why we assigned a bullish rating to the PIMCO 25+ Year Zero Coupon U.S. Treasury Index Exchange-Traded Fund ETF (NYSEARCA:ZROZ) a while back. This analysis covers a similar vehicle with slightly different key rate duration, namely the iShares 20+ Year Treasury Bond ETF (NASDAQ:TLT).

Without further delay, here's why we think TLT could present asymmetrical results for the foreseeable future.

Fund Overview

The iShares 20+ Year Treasury Bond ETF or TLT for short is a bucketed bond ETF, providing investors with thematic exposure to 20+ year treasury bonds. Note however that the vehicle's effective duration is 16.13, meaning it's more likely to react to 16 year key rates than 20+ year rates for now. The fund possesses a reasonable amount of convexity (3.53), enhancing upside possibilities while cushioning downside. In addition, TLT ETF has a negative option-adjusted spread, suggesting it's a hedge against credit risk.

Historical data shows that TLT ETF's income component is salient, likely due to its reliance on coupon income and not because of capital gain distributions. Nevertheless, the volatility embedded in the current market environment warrants close attention to capital gains/loss dynamics.

Market Commentary

A Systematic Argument Screams 'Buy'

As explained in two of last week's articles, we think a U.S. recession scenario is gathering pace. Some argue that tariffs will accelerate inflation, leading to higher bonds yields. However, we've assumed the opposite stance, arguing that the global trade war will diminish consumer sentiment, concurrently enhancing a recession scenario, especially as the U.S. economy is arguably in its late cycle. As an external reference, Goldman Sachs (GS) agrees with our outlook, as the investment bank recently assigned a 45% probability to its recession scenario analysis.

The U.S. yield curve has flattened in the past month. Considering the above-mentioned, we believe a reduction in level and possible inversion is inevitable. With that being said, 16-20 year key rates remain relatively pent-up, allowing investors to lock-in yields before lower yields materialise. For those unaware, yield reduction would likely lead to capital gains without sacrificing much coupon income (unless yields stay lower-for-longer). Thus, we see this as an incredible active opportunity for excess return-seeking investors.

Many might argue that a recession will lead to steepening of the curve. We agree with the notion. Though, our argument here is that the bond market will price a hard landing recession in advance, providing investors an active opportunity now instead of during a recession. In fact, we think it would be wise to ride intermediate-to-longer term yields into a recession and pivot into other opportunities once a recession has materialised. Alternatively, we'd start sliding into riskier assets in a deep recession scenario on the basis of "buy-the-dip."

However... Technical Aspects Might Restrict Systematic Support

Although we have reasonable confidence in our fundamental argument, technical aspects have to be considered before drawing a holistic conclusion. As illustrated below, the 20-year bid-to-cover ratio remains within range, suggesting ex-Fed open market supply/demand remains undeterred. TLT has traded flat year-over-year, therefore a change in supply/demand dynamics will likely need to occur for it to shift by an asymmetrical amount.

The SOMA, which communicates the New York Fed's open market operations, suggests lower treasury demand occurred in 2024. We anticipate the trajectory to change in 2025, yet, our opinion is merely an outlook and not necessarily the ground truth. Thus, a possibility remains that the supply/demand outlook at the Fed-level could remain docile for the time being.

Lastly, for this section. A glance at the Treasury Surplus echoes worries. Although the U.S. Fed would typically start buying bonds in a lowering-rate scenario, the deficit poses obstacles, suggesting the FED could finance rather than invest for the foreseeable future. Such a scenario could pose telling headwinds for TLT ETF from a supply/demand vantage point.

TLT's Key Metrics

I re-inserted the portfolio characteristics table for convenience. The data shows that TLT ETF has a 30-day SEC yield of 4.51%, about 4 basis points higher than ZROZ ETF. We find this comparison commendable; TLT has less duration exposure than ZROZ, yet its SEC yield remains higher. Moreover, TLT ETF has a yield-to-worst of approximately 4.53%, a compelling figure, suggesting the ETF has locked-in higher-than-normal yields for the time being.

In our view, an additional benefit of TLT ETF is its monthly distribution, allowing investors to lower their liquidity risk. In addition, the ETF has an expense ratio of 0.15%, which, according to our calculations, if compounded for ten years adds up to around 1.51%, a rather docile amount.

Conclusion

Considering the possibility of a hard landing, we think TLT ETF presents a unique opportunity to benefit from a sharp drop in the U.S. yield curve. In addition, the ETF has locked-in lucrative yields during a higher-for-longer interest rate environment. While we acknowledge risks such as supply/demand uncertainties and possible yield steepening during a recession, we see TLT ETF as an excellent active opportunity, especially if combined with ZROZ.
